turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This Court has never before, not once in its over 200-year history, required a state to turnover taxpayer funds to a house of worship. This case is quite remarkable. But the majorities does so today, holding that the Constitution requires Missouri to send a check drawing on public funds to the Trinity Lutheran Church. In doing so, it has made two mistakes, one with respect to each of the First Amendment's Religion Clauses. These two clauses, The Establishment Clause and The Free Exercise Clause, work in tandem to protect our liberty of conscience when it comes to religion. That is, they protect our right to choose any religious faith or to choose none at all. The Court's mistakes today diminish the protection the Religion Clauses provide to all of us. A full understanding of the facts of this case compels the conclusion that the Establishment Clause does not allow Missouri to send the check that the Trinity Lutheran Church wants. The Church sought state funding to resurface a playground attached to its child-learning center, a preschool and day-care on church property. In the church's own words, it uses the learning center to teach a Christian world view to children of members of the church as well as children of non-member residents of the area. This mission to use the learning center to spread the gospel is, again, in the church's own words, one of its sincere religious beliefs. The learning center's facilities, including the playground at issue in this case, exist to serve that mission. And so, what the church seeks from Missouri and what the majority today requires Missouri to provide is direct public funding for the facility the church uses to practice and spread its religious views. Our precedents have said over and over and over again that The Establishment Clause simply does not permit the use of public funds to finance religious activities in this way. Yet the Court mentions the Establishment Clause only to note the party's agreement that it does not prevent Missouri from including Trinity Lutheran in the Scrap Tire Program. The majority's silence on the issue signals that it has made one of two errors. For it to have accepted the party's concessions, it must either have concluded that the funding at issue here will not subsidize the church's religious activities. In which case, it has deeply misunderstood the facts of this case, or it must have departed dramatically from our precedents. The nature of the majority's error and its path will not be clear until the next case. The majority's second mistake lies in its discussion of The Free Exercise Clause. Missouri denied the church funding pursuant to Article 1, Section 7 of its Constitution which reflects a decision as a categorical matter not to fund houses of worship with public dollars. The Court today holds that the Free Exercise Clause prohibits that categorical decision. That holding is a radical one. Early in our nation's history, many states had what are called religious establishments; a system of laws and practices where the government sponsored, financially supported, and regulated religious activity. Every single state with a religious establishment took money from taxpayers and gave it to houses of worship and their ministries. In these states, a sustained public outcry led by both civic and religious leaders challenged these laws as incompatible with true freedom of conscience. The arguments made viewed public funding of core religious entities as distorting the free market for religion, involving government in a sphere where it did not belong, and forcing some taxpayers to violate their religious beliefs by funding religions they did not agree with. As a result, by 1933, every state, every single state had ended these laws as part of the process of disestablishing religion. Missouri has simply made the same choice as the one made over and over and over centuries ago. And today, that same choice is reflected in the constitutions of 30 other states. The Religion Clauses are designed to guard against the return to this earlier period of our history where freedom of conscience was a hope and not a guarantee. This is why, normally, we express great respect for the kind of sustained historical tradition of which Missouri's constitutional provision is a part. The majority does not just ignore this history. That would be bad enough. But it does so by creating a rigid rule not found in our precedents. It holds that the Free Exercise Clause does not allow the government to deny funding to religious entities based only on their religious status. But in this area of law, a decision to treat entities differently based on distinctions that the Religion Clauses make relevant does not amount to discrimination. The majority has decided to substitute its own view of what is best for religious liberty rather than to apply faithfully the view embodied in our history and in our precedents. Justice Ginsburg and I cannot join such a decision, and so we dissent.
